nodes.title: [2023-05-20 03:06] Parallax is the apparent displacement of an object when viewed from two different points
nodes.description: Parallax is the apparent displacement or shift in the position of an object when viewed from two different points, used in astronomy to determine the distance of objects that are too far away to measure directly, such as stars.

nodes.description: Astronomers used parallax to measure the distance of Regulus from Earth by observing the star from two points six months apart, using the distance between Earth's position in its orbit when it is closest to Regulus and when it is farthest away.

nodes.title: [2023-05-20 03:06] Regulus is approximately 79 light-years away from Earth
nodes.description: The precise measurements conducted by astronomers allowed them to determine that Regulus is approximately 79 light-years away from Earth.

nodes.title: [2023-05-20 03:06] Telescopes cannot directly measure the distance of stars

nodes.title: [2023-05-20 03:06] Parallax is the most accurate way to measure the distance of stars up to a few hundred light-years away
nodes.description: Parallax is currently the most accurate way to measure the distance of stars that are relatively close to us, up to a few hundred light-years away, compared to other methods that are less accurate.

nodes.title: [2023-05-20 03:06] Sending a spacecraft to measure the distance of stars is impractical
nodes.description: Sending a spacecraft to measure the distance of stars would be highly impractical, as even the nearest star, Proxima Centauri, is more than four light-years away, making it take more than four years to reach and return.

nodes.title: [2023-05-20 03:06] Parallax measurements can be done from Earth
nodes.description: Parallax measurements can be done from Earth, which is much more cost-effective than sending a spacecraft into deep space.
